title: Aipoola kaumualii Austin & Rubinoff 2026
author: Austin, Kyhl A.
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-zero
description: Aipoola kaumualii  Austin & Rubinoff, sp. nov.   http://zoobank.org/ urn:lsid:zoobank.org:act: FD765A78-FD5A-4148-8E02-CC4A8BED6FC0  Figs. 11A, B; 12A, D   Diagnosis.   Aipoola kaumualii  can be distinguished from other Hawaiian Tortricidae by the features listed above in the diagnosis of the genus.   Holotype,  ♂.  Kauaʻi:  Kōkeʻe State Park, Kōkeʻe Museum Area; 3800 ft [1158 m] / in silken tunnel under leaf of  Claoxylon  [ sandwicensis ]; larva coll. 3 vi 1982, [adult] em[erged] 28 vi 1982 / K. & E. Sattler / KAA diss. #1159 (♂) (NHMUK).   Paratypes  (11♂♂, 8♀♀):  Kauaʻi:  3♂♂, 6♀♀, same as holotype, except [adult] em[erged] 22–30 vi 1982 / KAA diss. #1160 (♀) (NHMUK). 2♂♂, Kōkeʻe State Park, Honopū Trail; 4000 ft [1219 m]; 4 vi 1982 / K. & E. Sattler (NHMUK). 2♂♂, Kōkeʻe State Park, Kahuamaʻa Flat; 4000 ft [1219 m]; 13 vi 1982 / K. & E. Sattler (NHMUK). 1♂, same as previous except 9 vi 1982 (NHMUK). 1♂, Kōkeʻe State Park, Kauaikinanā (Mohihi Road) 3600 ft [1097 m]; 1 vii 1982 / K. & E. Sattler (NHMUK). 1♂, Kōkeʻe State Park; 22.13085, -159.65881; 3679 ft [1121 m]; 22–23 vii 2013; R. Caesar / DNA extraction KA0085 / KAA diss. #0363 (UHIM). 1♀, Kōkeʻe Camp; 3600 ft [1097 m]; D. F. Hardwick / KAA diss. #0554 (CNC). 1♂, Kōkeʻe State Park, Awaʻawapuhi Trail, 22.1430, 159.6670; 1180 m; 21–22 iii 2022; K. A. Austin; LED [UV] bucket trap / DNA extraction KA0504 / KAA diss. #0749 (UHIM). 1♀, Kōkeʻe site B; 22.117°N, 159.670°W [22.1170, -159.6700]; 1100 m; 15 ix 2021; K. N. Magnacca; at light at night / K091523-08 (BPBM).   Description. Male (n=12).    Head.  Scaling on ventral half of frons pale yellow, dorsally directed, scaling on dorsal half long, brown, porrect; scaling on vertex yellowish brown, darker near frons; scaling on lateral surface of labial palpus pale yellowish brown, slightly darker brown scales occasionally present ventrally; scaling on medial surface pale yellow; scape with intermixed brown and yellowish brown scales; dorsal scales of flagellum entirely yellowish brown for first ten segments, then alternating rows of brown and yellowish brown scales beyond.  Thorax.  Scales on pronotum yellowish brown, with two latitudinal lines of darker brown scales dividing pronotum roughly into equal thirds; tegulae yellowish brown. Fore- and midleg with scaling on femur and tibia pale yellow, scaling on tarsus dark brown, ringed with pale yellow at joints; hindleg with scaling entirely pale yellow, a few scattered grey or brown scales present along lateral face of tarsus. Dorsal surface of FW (FWL: 9.3–9.9 mm; FAR: 2.2; Fig. 11A) with base of costa with small black scale patch; FW costal fold narrow, scaling yellowish brown, divided by small black scale patch; ground color of FW pale yellow, heavily overlaid with scattered yellowish brown, orange-brown, and black scales as follows: outer margin of basal fascia and inner margin of median fascia bounded by small patches of black and orange-brown scales; similar, smaller, scattered black and orange-brown scales present in distal third of FW; pale brown scales along veins in distal third of FW; six pairs of pale yellow strigulae present along costa, narrowing towards apex; fringe pale yellow. Dorsal surface of HW brown-yellow, with dense dark gray strigulae throughout; fringe light yellow on apical half, gray on anal margin. Ventral surface of FW gray, with scattered darker gray spots between veins, white patches along costa. Ventral surface of HW similar to dorsal surface but lighter ground color, resulting in a more highly contrasting appearance.  Abdomen.  Vestiture of abdomen with dorsal surface covered in dark gray scales, terminal 4–5 segments with a row of light yellow or yellow-brown scales along distal margin; scaling ventral surface pale yellow. Genitalia (Fig. 12A) as described for genus.   Description. Female (n=8).    Head.  Similar to male, but sensillae shorter.  Thorax.  Similar to male, but without costal fold, porrect costal scales present instead, costa more strongly arched; FW larger (FWL: 11.1–12.6 mm; FAR: 2.2; Fig. 11B), often slightly paler, banding less complete.   Abdomen .  Similar to male. Genitalia (Fig. 12D) as described for genus.   Etymology.  This species is named in honor of Kaumualiʻi, the last independent aliʻi nui (supreme chief) of Kauaʻi and Niʻihau before pledging allegiance to Kamehameha I and becoming a vassal within the unified Kingdom of Hawaiʻi in 1810.   Comments.  Despite  Aipoola  being a monotypic genus and only being collected for the first time in 1980, we have no reason to doubt the endemicity of  A .  kaumualii . Several other highly distinctive endemic genera have only been discovered in recent decades and were missed by early field biologists (e.g.,  Aumakua  [Noctuidae],  Kahakuloa  [Kahakuloaceae],  Melamprosops  [Fringillidae]).   Biology.  As described for genus.   Distribution.  As described for genus.   Conservation status.  Unknown. The host of  Aipoola kaumualii , poʻolā ( Claoxylon sandwicense ), is not as prevalent as it once was on Kauaʻi, but it can still be locally common in some locations in and around Kōkeʻe State Park. It is unclear why  A .  kaumualii  has been so rarely collected. It was never collected by early entomologists in Hawaiʻi, including Perkins, Swezey, and Zimmerman. Evidently, Klaus and Edith Sattler were the first to collect  A .  kaumualii  in 1982. Future surveys need to be undertaken to better understand the distribution and biology of  A .  kaumualii .
